title: Setting Fees As A Healer Without Guilt
dek: *The guilt you feel when naming your price isn't about greed or unworthiness—it's old anger turned inward, whispering that your care must come free. But healing work that depletes the healer serves no one. This is how to set fees that honor the exchange, hold the boundary, and let both parties show up whole.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Setting fees for healing work is a boundary practice that protects both you and your clients from resentment. Your pricing communicates the value of transformation and creates sustainable practice conditions. When you charge what your work is worth, you model self-respect for people learning to honor their own needs.

You've been told that if you truly cared, you wouldn't charge for it. That lie lands differently depending on who raised you, but the shape is always the same: real love is free, real service is sacrifice, and asking for money taints the purity of your gift. So you underprice. You barter yourself into exhaustion. You see one more client at a sliding scale that slides all the way to zero, and then you go home too tired to cook dinner, too depleted to return to your own practice, wondering why this path that once felt like a calling now feels like a slow hemorrhage. The guilt isn't about greed. It's about a boundary you were never taught to build. ## The Resentment That Lives in Underpricing In our experience, the practitioners who struggle most with fee-setting are the ones who do the deepest work. You know what it costs - not in dollars, but in presence, in the emptying-out that happens when you hold space for someone else's pain. You know that after a session, you need twenty minutes of silence, a walk, maybe the grounding sweetness of [Real Mushrooms Reishi Capsules (90 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0B21NBL5T?tag=spankyspinola-20) to bring your nervous system back to center. And yet you charge $60 for ninety minutes. Beverly Engel, who has written extensively on the relationship between boundaries and anger, points out that chronic under-giving to ourselves creates a kind of simmering resentment that we then project onto the very people we're trying to serve. When you don't charge enough, you start to resent your clients for taking what you offered. You resent the appointment reminder. You resent their slowness to change, their resistance, their need. None of this is their fault. They're simply accepting the terms you set. The anger isn't at them. It's at the invisible contract you signed somewhere in childhood: that your worth is measured by how much you give away. ## What a Fee Actually Does A fee isn't a toll. It's a threshold. When someone pays for your time, they're making a commitment that free advice never requires. They're saying, with their wallet and their calendar, that this matters. Thomas Moore, in his reflections on soul and work, suggests that money in a therapeutic or healing exchange isn't merely transactional - it's transformational. It marks the boundary between casual conversation and sacred work. It asks both parties to take the encounter seriously. You've probably noticed this in your own practice. The clients who pay full price show up on time. They do the homework. They're less likely to cancel at the last minute or ghost after two sessions. This isn't about moral superiority - it's about skin in the game. When the exchange is clear, the work can go deeper. And on your end, when you're fairly compensated, you can show up without the background hum of resentment. You can be present. You can listen without half your attention on the bills you're not paying, the time you're not spending with your own family, the other work you had to turn down to make space for this session at a price that doesn't sustain you. ## The Nervous System Math of Saying Your Number Here's what happens in your body when you're about to name your fee: your heart rate ticks up. Your palms might sweat. You feel a tightness in your throat, a flutter in your belly. If you've spent time with the NIH overview of anger and the autonomic nervous system, you'll recognize this as a classic sympathetic response - your body preparing for conflict, rejection, or shame. This is old wiring. Somewhere in your history, asking for what you need was punished. Maybe not with violence, but with withdrawal, with coldness, with the message that you were selfish or demanding. So now, years later, your nervous system treats every fee conversation like a threat. The work isn't to eliminate the fear. It's to name your price anyway. In my own practice, I started by writing my fee on a card and keeping it in my pocket during intake calls. When the moment came, I pulled it out and read it aloud, word for word: "My rate is $150 for a sixty-minute session." I didn't apologize. I didn't offer a discount in the same breath. I just let the number sit there, and I breathed. Most of the time, people said yes. Sometimes they asked about a sliding scale, and I had an answer ready. But the sky didn't fall. And over time, my nervous system learned that I could survive stating my worth. ## Building Your Fee Structure Without Apologizing You don't need to justify your pricing with a dissertation on your credentials. You don't need to compare yourself to therapists or yoga teachers or the Reiki practitioner down the street. You need to do some basic math, and then you need to honor it. Start here: How much do you need to earn per month to live without financial panic? Include rent, food, insurance, student loans, the small things that keep you human - your [herbal cabinet](/herbs), your books, the occasional night out. Divide that by the number of sessions you can realistically offer without burning out. Most healers can sustain 15-20 client-facing hours per week before their own reserves start to drain. Add in prep time, admin, continuing education. Now add 20% for taxes if you're self-employed, and another 10% for the months when people cancel or you get sick. That's your number. It might be higher than you expected. It might make your stomach clench. But it's not arbitrary. It's what you actually need. From there, you can build in a small number of sliding-scale spots if you want to, but make them genuinely limited. Three per month, maybe. Not half your practice. And make the criteria clear - not just "whoever asks," but a specific income threshold or circumstance. This keeps the boundary intact while still making space for accessibility. ## The Language of the Exchange How you talk about money matters. Not because you're trying to manipulate anyone, but because clarity is kindness. Avoid: "I usually charge $120, but I could maybe do $80 if that's easier for you?" Try: "My standard rate is $120 per session. I hold three sliding-scale spots each month for people experiencing financial hardship. If that applies to you, the range is $80-$100. Let me know what works, and we'll go from there." Notice the difference. The first version is an apology. It tells the potential client that your rate is negotiable, that you don't really believe in it yourself. The second version is clean information. It names the structure, offers the option, and then stops talking. Thich Nhat Hanh teaches that right speech is speech that doesn't create suffering - for others or for yourself. When you waffle on your pricing, you create suffering. You create confusion for the client, who now has to guess what you really want. And you create resentment for yourself, because you've just agreed to something that doesn't serve you. State your rate. Breathe. Let them respond. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Fee Conversations You can do this right now, before your next intake call or email exchange. Sit down with a piece of paper. Write your fee at the top - just the number, no explanation. Now write three sentences: 1. What this fee allows me to do in my life. 2. What this fee allows me to offer my clients. 3. What I'm no longer willing to sacrifice. For example: - This fee lets me work four days a week instead of six, so I have time for my own spiritual practice. - This fee means I can show up rested and present, not scrambling between sessions to make rent. - I'm no longer willing to sacrifice my health to prove I'm a good person. Read these sentences aloud. Feel them in your body. Let them be true. The next time someone asks your rate, take a breath and picture this paper. You're not defending a number. You're honoring a life. ## When Guilt Shows Up Anyway It will. Even after you've done the math, even after you've set the boundary, even after three clients have paid your full rate without blinking - guilt will tap you on the shoulder. You'll see someone's face fall when you name your price. You'll get an email from someone who can't afford you, and they'll tell you their story, and it will be heartbreaking. You'll wonder if you're gatekeeping healing, if you've become the very thing you set out to dismantle. Let me tell you what I've seen over the years: the people who most need your work will find a way if they're ready. And the people who aren't ready will use your price as the reason, but it's never really the price. It's timing. It's readiness. It's the hundred invisible factors that determine whether someone is prepared to do deep work. Your job isn't to make healing free. Your job is to offer it with integrity, in a structure that doesn't destroy you. If you want to make your work more accessible, there are ways that don't involve personal bankruptcy. You can offer group sessions. You can create a sliding-scale fund that clients can donate to. You can teach, write, record - [more articles in this journal](/articles) explore these models in detail. But one-on-one depletion is not a social justice strategy. It's just burnout with better marketing. ## The Clients Who Teach You This There will be one. Maybe more than one. A client who pays your rate without hesitation, who shows up early, who does the work between sessions, who sends you a note six months later to say that what you did together changed their life. And you'll realize: they didn't balk at your fee because they understood something you're still learning. They understood that transformation has a cost, and that cost isn't just financial. It's time. It's attention. It's the willingness to be uncomfortable in service of becoming whole. When you charge appropriately, you're selecting for people who are ready to meet you in that commitment. You're not keeping anyone out. You're inviting in the ones who can actually use what you're offering. Over the years I've seen practitioners double their rates and find that their practice gets *better* - not because expensive sessions work better, but because the people who show up at that price point are the ones who've already decided to change. They're not shopping. They're not testing. They're ready. ## Common Questions About Healer Fees ### How do I know if I'm charging too much? You're not charging too much if people are booking sessions and the work is sustainable for you. The market will tell you - if no one books for three months, reassess. But one or two people saying no isn't data. It's just preference. Your fee should allow you to work 15-20 hours a week maximum in direct client care and still meet your basic needs. If you're working 40 hours and still broke, your rate is too low. ### What if someone I care about can't afford me? Then you make a choice - not a default. You can offer them a limited number of sessions at a reduced rate, with a clear end date. You can refer them to [the assessments hub](/assessments) for self-guided resources. You can offer to barter if the exchange is genuinely equal and you actually want what they're offering. But don't quietly resent them while pretending it's generosity. That poisons the work for both of you. ### Should I post my rates publicly or only share them in conversation? Both approaches work, and the choice depends on your comfort and your market. Public rates save time and filter for people who are already in your range. Private rates let you have a conversation first and offer nuance. I'd suggest starting with a range posted publicly - "Sessions start at $120" - and then having the full conversation when someone reaches out. This respects both transparency and flexibility. --- You'll know you've arrived at right pricing when you can say your number without your voice going up at the end, without the little laugh that means "just kidding, I'll take less." When you can let silence follow your rate and not rush to fill it with justification. The healers who last - who are still doing this work at 50, at 60, with depth and presence and joy - are the ones who learned early that care and clarity aren't opposites. They're partners. Your fee is part of your care. It's the boundary that lets you keep showing up, year after year, for the people who are ready to meet you there. And for a beautiful exploration of how emotions - including the anger that masquerades as guilt - carry wisdom when we're willing to listen, [The Language of Emotions - Karla McLaren](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1583949968?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a framework that has shaped how many of us understand feeling as information rather than obstacle. The work you do matters. The price you charge doesn't diminish that. It sustains it. And on the days when the guilt is loud, when you're second-guessing everything, consider stepping into some of the practices we share over at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) - or even just making yourself a cup of tea with a drop of [Plant Therapy Organic Lavender (30 mL)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00LABX3DY?tag=spankyspinola-20) to calm the noise. Then come back to the number you wrote down, the one that lets you live and work and stay whole, and trust it. Your clarity is a gift. Your boundary is medicine. And the people who need you will find their way. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on setting fees as a healer without guilt, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
